name: MLKit — Tofte-Talpin Region Inference Implementation
summary: MLKit is the canonical 'no annotations, all inferred' baseline for region-based memory management. It implements Tofte-Talpin's stack-of-regions semantics for Standard ML using Algorithm R (TOPLAS 1998). Recent work combines region inference with tag-free generational GC (JFP 2021) and parallelises inference itself (PLDI 2023).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**MLKit** is the canonical "no annotations, all inferred" baseline for region-based memory management. It implements Tofte-Talpin's stack-of-regions semantics (1994; ICFP 1996; IC 1997) for Standard ML: a region-annotated lambda calculus where every value lives in a region, regions form a runtime stack, and `letregion ρ in e` is the only allocation/deallocation primitive.

The sister record `data/mlkit-regionexp` covers the IR/representation angle — the typed-region annotated lambda calculus the compiler operates over. This record covers the implementation, the inference algorithm, and the modern (2021–2023) extensions to the MLKit story.

sections.title: Tofte-Talpin theory and region/effect polymorphism
sections.content:
Tofte-Talpin's stack-of-regions semantics is a region-annotated lambda calculus where every value lives in a region, regions form a runtime stack, and `letregion ρ in e` is the only allocation/deallocation primitive. **Region- and effect-polymorphism with polymorphic recursion in regions** is the load-bearing extension: type schemes ∀α…ρ…ε.τ are required for recursive functions to allocate into different regions per call.

Without polymorphic recursion in regions, recursive functions would have to share a single region for all call levels, which would either pin too much data live or force a uniform deallocation point too late. Polymorphic recursion lets each recursive call materialise a fresh region variable in its scheme.

sections.title: Algorithm R and the leak problem
sections.content:
Algorithm specification: unification + constraint-solving. Mads Tofte / Martin Elsman's syntax-directed **Algorithm R** (TOPLAS 1998) uses Algorithm-W-style unification with fixed-point iteration to handle polymorphic-recursive regions; Birkedal-Tofte's alternative is constraint-based. MLKit ships the syntax-directed one. **Storage-mode / multiplicity inference** further refines representations after inference.

Annotation burden: zero; inference power: very strong, with classic pathologies (region "stuck" until end of program — the **leak problem**, partly fixed by Aiken-Fähndrich-Levien 1995's relaxed LIFO, which lets regions die earlier than the syntactic scope that introduced them when usage allows).

sections.title: Regions + GC and parallel region inference
sections.content:
Two recent extensions update the 1990s MLKit story to a multicore world.

**Elsman & Hallenberg (JFP 2021)** combine region inference with tag-free generational GC: per-region collectors are specialised by exploiting *typed regions*, and write barriers are minimised. Empirically the combination often beats either technique alone — regions handle the common case (short-lived data freed in bulk), GC handles the pathological case (long-lived data with complex sharing). Status (as of 2026-04): production-strong in the MLKit distribution.

**Elsman & Henriksen (PLDI 2023)** deliver parallel region inference for MLKit with leaf threads, avoiding allocation races during inference itself. This extends the practical applicability of region inference to multicore-era compilation.
